teamObjective.explanation: Partner is down to 2 cards and near finishing. Leading the cheapest possible single keeps our slightly better diamonds in reserve and gives partner the lowest bar to win or shed a card without being forced to burn a high card.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) likely holds a small hand aimed at going out. They may have a modest winner or at least a low diamond they can shed, but their exact cards are hidden.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents together hold 6 hidden cards and have not shown strength. They probably can beat a low diamond with a higher diamond or trump, but no specific holdings are known.

actionRationale.primaryReason: play-single-diamond-3-copy1 is the minimum commitment. It keeps 4, 5, 6 for later, makes it cheapest for partner to take the trick if possible, and avoids wasting our strongest card when the whole hand is weak.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Only marginally stronger than the 3 and still likely beaten; it removes a potentially useful intermediate card without changing the trick-winning odds much.
Expends a middle card from an already weak hand. If opponents take the trick, we are left with even lower remaining cards.
Uses our highest non-trump. Even if it wins, being left on lead with 3-4-5 and no trumps is worse than letting opponents spend a card to take this trick.

risks: An opponent can beat the 3 with a higher diamond or trump and seize the lead.
Partner may be forced to play a trump or high card to contest, slowing their finish.
We have no trumps or bombs, so we cannot reclaim the lead easily.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the absolute minimum minimizes the cost of losing the trick and preserves our better low cards for future follow opportunities.

notes: No public plays have occurred, so all beliefs are prior-based and grounded only in hand counts and the new-game event.
